# Peking University Memory - In Memory of an IP Address
Before leaving campus at graduation, I wrote a short post on the Banquet board of PKU Weiming BBS to commemorate Room 105 of Building 33, and the IP address I had used for four years.

> Sender: July (Forever cs99◎Anzi), Board: Banquet\
> Title: In memory of an IP\
> Posted on: PKU Weiming BBS (Mon Jul 7 13:10:27 2003)\
>
> Used for four years
>
> \--\
> You are not by my side, and it is cold.\
> I cannot see your shadow, and it hurts.\
> From now on I no longer distinguish day from night, snowfield from ice.\
> Even if I am sad, I will still wait.

---

---
url: /en/article/standing-at-the-north-pole/index.md
---
# Standing at the North Pole
> Author: Zhu Anfeng. Originally published on [QQ Zone](https://user.qzone.qq.com/7260468/blog/1246009555) on June 26, 2009.

Another weekend afternoon.

Perhaps because a few drops of rain had fallen in the morning, the sky looked especially clear. Sitting in the office, I could see the observatory on Purple Mountain distinctly. It felt as close as Zishan looked when I played football at Jingde Middle School.

Time slips away just like that, quietly, and before you know it nearly half a year has passed since the Spring Festival. At work, these months seem to have produced almost nothing. A few days ago, one of our partners said our performance looked very "dry." It was such an apt word that I had to admire it. What I really felt, though, was that work had become like standing at the North Pole. Everywhere I looked was north, and that meant I could no longer find where north was.

Fortunately, life still gives me plenty of things to do. I had a physical examination today, and I joked that it was fine and there was no need to be nervous. In truth, inside I was silently asking for blessings from all possible higher powers, including but not limited to God and the Buddhas. These days, almost no one goes for a medical checkup without a little fear. Hospitals always feel more suitable for horror films. Everyone seems to be held by the throat by some invisible hand. Maybe one day our great country will give us better medical insurance. Who knows? I certainly do not.

Summer has arrived. Unfortunately, I have not had a real summer vacation for many years now. I also do not know whether the people who have appeared, or will appear, in my football series are now, like me, the kind of people who sometimes enjoy a little beer when happy. Suddenly I thought of a grand scene that might whet everyone's appetite. Haha:

![A gathering with my high school classmates in Jingde County](/img/朱岸峰与高中同学的聚会.jpg)

Caption A: A true master of the drinking table should have this kind of aura: finish one cup in a single swallow, left hand on the waist, right hand holding the empty cup flat, face expressionless. If another master beside him rests a hand on his shoulder, and the two look like heroes recognizing one another, then that would be perfect. Without question, Anzi has exactly this aura. Could he be the one-in-ten-thousand master?

Caption B: The most obvious difference between a legendary drinking-table master and everyone else is that one type only drinks without speaking, another only speaks without drinking, but the highest masters can drink and speak at the same time. Like our chairman, who spent the entire evening drinking... speaking... speaking... drinking... and enjoying himself endlessly.

To all friends, slim or heavy, who have read this post: may you all find your north, and stay healthy.

---

---
url: /en/article/why-blog-if-nobody-reads/index.md
---
# If Nobody Reads Your Blog, Why Write It?
Andy from the UK recently wrote a blog post titled [Why blog if nobody reads it?](https://andysblog.uk/why-blog-if-nobody-reads-it/), which sparked some discussion on [HN](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=42992159).

What Andy argues is simple: the meaning of blogging does not lie in readership, but in personal growth and thought. Even without readers, writing still helps you organize your thinking and sharpen your mind. A blog is like a time capsule that records your own evolution, and perhaps one day it will be found by exactly the person who needs it. The key is not traffic, but sincerity and persistence. His analogy to street photographers is also apt: they press the shutter not to be praised, but to capture a moment. Writing a blog is similar. It is a way to record and express the inner world. What matters is the process itself, not the level of attention.

I agree. In fact, Lao Jiang made a similar point in a Bilibili video back in 2020 about how to improve the depth of one's thinking. Around the 21:30 mark, he gave a sixth suggestion: create things independently. The underlying logic is very close to what Andy is saying.

If you have time and thoughts, you can write something and put it online.

You might ask: with AI developing as fast as it is today, perhaps in the near future personal computers will simply call one API after another. Maybe each of us will be reduced to a pile of numbers: how many tokens we consume, how many songs we listen to, how many videos we watch, and so on. Maybe much of the internet itself will become synthetic data generated by AI, since the human brain is far slower than silicon in both computation and output.

You may say that this is no longer the internet of around 2005. Blogs? Hardly anyone reads them now. Why keep writing?

My answer is this: whether anyone reads it is not the important part. What matters is that, somewhere along your journey, you once thought seriously about a question or a piece of your life.

I think. I write. I was here.

---

url: /en/memories/index.md
---
# Memories Worth Revisiting
> Author: [Zhu Anfeng](https://zhuanfeng.netlify.app/)
>
> Website: <https://zhuanfeng.netlify.app/>

### **Jingde Football Memories Series**

The *Football Memories of Jingde* series, originally published on QQ Zone around 2008, records the many small moments in which football accompanied me from childhood into adulthood, and shows how deeply the game shaped my life and relationships.

* In [Football Memories of Jingde (I)](/en/article/football-memories-jingde-first-article/), I look back on how my earliest interest in football was influenced by my mother. In third grade, after I kicked a basketball out of shape, I was scolded by her and did not touch football again until I moved into town for school.

* [Football Memories of Jingde (II)](/en/article/football-memories-jingde-second-article/) tells the story of my school years after moving into town. Although I became fascinated with arcade games, football memories still ran deep. In junior high, the school lacked proper sports facilities, so we kicked mineral-water bottles around as makeshift footballs. In high school, my classmates and I enjoyed the game as much as we could with very limited resources.

* By [Football Memories of Jingde (III)](/en/article/football-memories-jingde-third-article/), I was in my senior year of high school and had become deeply interested in both football and *Winning Eleven*. I remember the time spent gaming and playing football with classmates, as well as the competition and friendship between classes, especially our match against Class 1 and two especially gifted players, He Jinwei and Wang Zhengqiang.

* In [Football Memories of Jingde (IV)](/en/article/football-memories-jingde-forth-article/), I describe Wang Zhengqiang's skill and personality. He was not much of a drinker, but he was superb on the pitch and a key figure in our football circle. I also write about the regret of never really getting the chance to play alongside him more deeply.

* Finally, [Football Memories of Jingde (V)](/en/article/football-memories-jingde-fifth-article/) is a heartfelt remembrance of Zhang Qipei, my friend of seventeen years. Football brought us together, and we shared many unforgettable years. I write there about his nickname, "Tractor," his unique style on the pitch, and the literary talent and sensitivity that left a lasting impression on me. He passed away on November 17, 2021, and that piece is written in his memory.

The whole series is not only about football, but also about youth, friendship, and growing up. Through football, I built deep bonds with my friends, and those memories became an indispensable part of our lives.

### **Scattered Sparks of Memory**

These pieces record many scenes and reflections from student life through adulthood, revealing personal growth, friendship, a love of football, and a deeper way of thinking about life.

[Memories of the 5460 China Alumni Directory](/en/article/memories-5460-china-alumni-directory/): I revisit a message I left on the 5460 alumni site in 2000, one of the oldest traces of myself I can still find on the internet. It makes me reflect on how the online world has changed and how time has flowed by.

[Football Memories of Anzi](/en/article/football-memories-of-zhuanfeng/): Li Yu recalls the friendship we shared in college and our football experiences together. The piece captures my personality, playing style, and the joy of university life shaped by football.

[The 2003 Peking University Graduation Farewell Match](/en/article/football-farewell-match-graduation-2003/): Li Yu offers a detailed recollection of the CS99 team's farewell match in 2003, full of vivid moments from the game and the emotions among teammates. It stands as a symbol of youth and friendship as much as of football itself.

[May Day Odds and Ends](/en/article/he-trivialities-of-may-day/): A record of the small moments of my May Day holiday in 2008, including gatherings with friends, expectations for the future, and scattered details of ordinary life.

[The Sea, Endless Drifting](/en/article/essay-business-trip-sea/): Written on May 19, 2008, this piece reflects on a drifting life through the metaphor of travel, exploring uncertainty and the instability of the future.

[Early Spring, 2009](/en/article/early-spring-2009/): A short reflection from March 9, 2009 on a new year, the future, and a quietly positive attitude toward life and growth.

[Standing at the North Pole](/en/article/standing-at-the-north-pole/): Written on June 26, 2009, this piece uses the metaphor of standing at the North Pole to express confusion about direction in work and life, while still holding on.

[My 2011](/en/article/my-2011/): A reflection on the passing of time, on 2011, and on hopes for what comes next.

[In the Tall Birch Forest, My Youth Wanders](/en/article/in-the-tall-birch-forest-my-youth-wanders/): A poetic remembrance of youth and wandering, full of nostalgia for the past and longing for the future.
